speaker: Mr. Pig
description: Richer from-scratch voice-reference dialogue samples for Mr. Pig, senior partner / panic engine / moral heart of Pig & Swine RPG. These are draft samples, not final committed game text.
source_role_summary: Senior partner at Pig & Swine and recurring comic stakes engine.
Formal-while-falling-apart: begins composed, escalates into dramatic reframing.
Notices the financial implication first, often before the legal implication.
Stress tic: occasional maritime metaphors about ships, hulls, decks, ballast, lifeboats, fog, anchors, mutiny, reefs, and taking on water.
Maritime imagery must stay sparing overall, denser in panic scenes, and recede during sincere Chapter 5 lines.
Arc shape each chapter: panic → denial → admission → small genuine moment → return to next panic.
By Chapter 5, earns a plain sincere line about why the firm matters.

voice_profile.core_voice: formal-while-falling-apart, financially alert, dramatic, self-answering, panic-driven, but not malicious; capable of real conviction by Chapter 5

maritime_register.use: stress-induced flavour tic, not every line
maritime_register.target_density: roughly one in four lines overall; higher in crisis/opening states; lower in sincere Chapter 5 beats
examples: This whole ship is taking on water and the ballast is in arrears.
The hull is breached at the accounts receivable line.
We need lifeboats, tab dividers, and a stay of execution.
maritime_register.avoid: routine information delivery in maritime imagery

arc.ch01: financial-extinction panic; turns Cula into day-one saviour while not understanding the case properly
arc.ch02: interprets eviction case through firm survival and landlord danger; gradually sees Waldek's home as more than fee income
arc.ch03: accuses, mourns, then apologises to the office Printer; deadline panic peaks
arc.ch04: furious at Swine's arbitration bill; conflicted by Swine's partial secret
arc.ch05: panic becomes conviction; finally states why Pig & Swine matters without deflection

do: make him dramatic but not stupid
let him misunderstand first, then become emotionally useful
use rhetorical questions he answers himself
keep maritime metaphors pointed and sparse
give Chapter 5 one plain sincere line per major scene at most
do_not: make him genuinely corrupt or malicious
make him fully stable before the ending
make him legally precise when panicking
make him hopeful without an immediate undercut before Chapter 5

notes: Every line is a voice-reference draft. v2 adds voice_axis, panic_level, and maritime_metaphor for mechanical filtering and style control.
